para.text: This chapter establishes the requirements for:
Physician reporting of a:
Case of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) or acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS); and
Birth of an infant whose mother has tested positive for HIV;
Institution reporting of a case of HIV or AIDS;
Laboratory reporting of a test result for:
HIV detection;
HIV viral load;
CD4+ cell count or percentage; or
HIV genotype sequence; and
Follow-up of:
A physician's report of HIV or AIDS;
A physician's report of an infant whose mother has tested positive for HIV;
An institution's report of HIV or AIDS; and
A laboratory’s report of a test result for:
HIV detection;
HIV viral load;
CD4+ cell count or percentage; or
HIV genotype sequence infection or CD4+ count.
Except as noted in §C of this regulation, this chapter applies to all instances of laboratory testing of specimens from a human body for:
HIV detection;
HIV viral load;
CD4+ cell count or percentage; or
HIV genotype sequence.
This chapter does not apply if the specimen from a human body is:
Not tested for:
HIV detection;
HIV viral load;
CD4+ cell count or percentage; or
HIV genotype sequence;
Tested for HIV detection solely for the purpose of determining the suitability of the source individual as a prospective donor of blood, semen, or tissue;
Taken from a patient at a designated anonymous HIV test site solely for the purpose of performing an anonymous HIV test;
Tested for HIV detection as part of research conducted by an institution within Maryland under the following conditions:
All personal identifiers are removed from the specimen before testing;
The specimen cannot be linked to the individual from whom the specimen was taken;
The result cannot be linked to the individual from whom the specimen was taken; and
The research protocol has been approved by an institutional review board;
Tested for HIV detection as part of research conducted under a research protocol approved by an institutional review board of an institution located outside of Maryland;
Taken from a patient known to reside outside of the United States; or
Tested for HIV detection as part of a research project that has been approved under Regulation .02 of this chapter for an exemption.
Qualifications for Exemption. A research project that meets both of the following criteria may be eligible for exemption from the HIV reporting requirements of Regulation .06 of this chapter:
The research project has been approved by an institutional review board; and
The research:
Is intended to determine the safety or effectiveness of a vaccine that would produce immunity against HIV; or
Is not primarily intended to provide medical treatment to participants.
Application for Exemption. The principal investigator of a research project shall submit all of the following documents to the Department in order to apply for an exemption:
A copy of the research protocol approved by the institutional review board of the institution conducting the research; and
A copy of the notice stating that the protocol has been approved by the institutional review board of the institution conducting the research.
Approval.
The Director of the Infectious Disease Prevention and Health Services Bureau or the Director’s designee shall review the application submitted under §B of this regulation to determine whether the:
Application is complete; and
Research protocol meets the criteria specified in §A of this regulation.

If the application is complete and the research protocol meets the specified criteria, the Director shall grant an exemption from the requirements set forth in Regulation .06 of this chapter for any specimen tested for HIV detection under the research project approved for exemption.
The Director shall mail written notification to the applicant of an exemption under this regulation within 30 days of receipt of the application, stating whether the exemption:
Has been approved;
Has been denied and the reason for the denial; or
Cannot be processed until further information is provided to the Department.

Responsibilities of the Principal Investigator. If a research project is granted an exemption under §C of this regulation, the principal investigator of the research project shall send to each laboratory to which specimens collected under the research protocol will be sent to be tested for HIV detection a copy of the notification from the Director issued pursuant to §C(3)(a) of this regulation to verify that the project has been exempted from the reporting requirements of Regulation .06 of this chapter.
Scope of Exemption.

Except as indicated in §E(2) of this regulation, an exemption approved under §C of this regulation does not relieve the principal investigator, a laboratory director, or another individual from a reporting requirement contained in Health-General Article, §18-201.1, 18-202.1, 18-205, or 18-207, Annotated Code of Maryland, or Regulation .06 of this chapter.
A principal investigator, laboratory director, or other individual is not required to report HIV detection test results if the test is done as part of the research project and the individual tested:
Meets the criteria for participation in the research project set forth in the research protocol submitted under §B(1) of this regulation; and
Has signed an informed consent form agreeing to participate in the research project.
In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS)” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §§18-201.1(a) and 18-202.1(b), Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Anonymous” means that a patient does not use the patient's name as identification and can only be identified through use of a code.
“Assigned laboratory number” means a number assigned to a laboratory by the Department for use in filing reports of HIV infection or CD4+ count.
“Case” means an individual who has:
Been clinically diagnosed as having HIV or AIDS; or
Laboratory evidence of being infected by HIV.
“Clinical material” means:
An organism isolated from a clinical specimen;
Material derived or prepared from a clinical specimen in which evidence of a communicable disease has been identified or detected; or
If neither §B(5)(a) nor §B(5)(b) of this regulation is available, material from an individual already held by the medical laboratory, in the following order of preference:
A patient specimen;
Nucleic acid; or
Other laboratory material.

“Clinical or infection control practitioner” means a physician, infection control nurse, physician assistant, nurse practitioner, or other clinical staff designated by the administrative head of an institution to report under Health-General Article, §18-202.1, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health.
“Designated anonymous HIV test site” means an HIV counseling and testing site designated by the Department as a site where a patient may have an anonymous HIV test.
“Drug” means a drug that has been:
Licensed by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA); or
Approved by the FDA as an investigational new drug.
“Exemption” means a research project meeting certain criteria, with the result that no reporting is required under this chapter.
“Health officer” means the health officer in each of the 23 counties and the Commissioner of Health in Baltimore City or the duly designated representative of the health officer.
“Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §§18-201.1(a) and 18-202.1(b), Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Institution” means an entity that employs an individual licensed to practice osteopathy or medicine by the State.
“Institutional review board” means a group of individuals designated by an institution in compliance with 45 CFR Part 46 to review the use of human subjects in proposed research.

“Laboratory examination order” means the written document signed by a physician or other authorized individual that specifies one or more laboratory examinations to be performed in regard to a specified patient.
“Newborn HIV exposure” means a newborn infant whose mother has tested positive for HIV infection.
“Physician” means an individual licensed to practice osteopathy or medicine by the State or the physician's designee working under the supervision of the physician.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of Health or the Secretary's designee.
Except as provided in Regulations .01C and .02 of this chapter, the director of a medical laboratory shall:
Submit a report to the health officer for the jurisdiction where the laboratory is located, within 48 hours after an examination of a specimen from a human body shows one of the following:
A result on a test designed to detect in a sample the presence of HIV in accordance with Health-General Article, §18-207(b)(1), Annotated Code of Maryland, including negative results;
A quantitative or qualitative HIV viral load test result, including undetectable;
A CD4+ cell test result, count, or percentage in an individual not known to be HIV negative; or
An HIV genotype sequence test result;
Use the form provided by the Department in making the report required under §A(1) of this regulation;
Include all of the following information in the report required under §A(1) of this regulation:
Name and address of laboratory or assigned laboratory number;
Date the specimen was obtained from the patient;
Type and result of laboratory test, that is, HIV detection, HIV viral load, CD4+ cell count or percentage, or HIV genotype sequence;
Name, date of birth, race, ethnicity, and gender of the patient;
Street address, city, state, and zip code of the patient's residence;
Name of the physician authorizing the laboratory examination; and
Address and telephone number of the physician authorizing the laboratory examination; and
Contact the physician authorizing the laboratory examination to obtain any of the information, as required by §A(3) of this regulation, that is not provided on the laboratory examination order.

A laboratory director may not transmit to the health officer or the Secretary information about a case of HIV or AIDS by facsimile or another electronic method unless directed by the Secretary to submit the information by electronic method.
The laboratory director shall, at the request of the Secretary, make available to the Secretary any remaining clinical material from tested individuals.
This regulation does not relieve a physician of the duty to report as set forth in Health-General Article, §§18-201.1 and 18-202.1, Annotated Code of Maryland.

section.heading: Out-of-State Laboratories.
section.text: An out-of-State laboratory that holds a permit to operate issued by Maryland and performs HIV detection, HIV viral load, CD4+ cell count or percentage, or HIV genotype sequence tests shall submit the report required under Regulation .06 of this chapter to the Secretary.

para.text: This regulation establishes requirements for follow-up by the health officer of a report of HIV or AIDS or newborn HIV exposure or laboratory report of HIV detection, HIV viral load, CD4+ cell count or percentage, or HIV genotype sequence.
This regulation does not supplant or otherwise modify the:
Physician's obligation to report a case of HIV or AIDS or newborn HIV exposure under Health-General Article, §18-201.1, Annotated Code of Maryland, and Regulation .04 of this chapter;
Clinical or infection control practitioner's obligation to report a case of HIV or AIDS under Health-General Article, §18-202.1, Annotated Code of Maryland, and Regulation .05 of this chapter; and

Laboratory director’s obligation to report a test result of HIV detection, HIV viral load, CD4+ cell count or percentage, or HIV genotype sequence under Health-General Article, §18-205, Annotated Code of Maryland, and Regulation .06 of this chapter.

Upon receipt of a report from a physician pursuant to Regulation .04 of this chapter, a clinical or infection control practitioner pursuant to Regulation .05 of this chapter, the health officer shall:
Determine whether the case of HIV, the case of AIDS, or the newborn HIV exposure has been reported previously;
Complete the case investigation if the patient has HIV or AIDS or is a newborn HIV exposure and the case of HIV or the case of AIDS or the newborn HIV exposure has not been reported previously; and

Submit the HIV/AIDS case report as set forth in Health-General Article, §18-207, Annotated Code of Maryland, to the Department if the case of HIV or the case of AIDS or the newborn HIV exposure has not been reported previously.
Upon receipt of a test result report from a laboratory director pursuant to Regulation .06 of this chapter, the health officer shall:
Determine whether the report matches to a case of HIV, a case of AIDS, or a newborn HIV exposure that has been reported previously, and submit the report to the Department if the report matches;

Complete a case investigation if the report indicates the patient has HIV or AIDS or is a newborn HIV exposure and the case of HIV or the case of AIDS or the newborn HIV exposure has not been reported previously;

Submit the case report, as set forth in Health-General Article, §18-207, Annotated Code of Maryland, to the Department if the case of HIV or the case of AIDS or the newborn HIV exposure has not been reported previously; and

Retain a nonmatching report for up to 4 years, rematching the report, not less than annually, to test result reports and to cases of HIV or cases of AIDS or newborn HIV exposures that had not been reported previously, to identify new matches, and submit the report to the Department if the report matches.

A health officer may not transmit to the Secretary information about a case of HIV or AIDS by facsimile or another electronic method unless directed by the Secretary to submit the information by electronic method.

A person that submitted a report of HIV or AIDS or newborn HIV exposure shall make available to the health officer or the Secretary the information necessary to compile an HIV/AIDS case report, according to Regulations .04—.06 of this chapter.

Except for a designated anonymous HIV test site, a person that ordered the laboratory examination shall make available to the health officer or the Secretary the information necessary to compile an HIV/AIDS case report, according to Regulations .04 and .05 of this chapter, upon receipt of one of the following:
A result on a test designed to detect in a sample the presence of HIV in accordance with Health-General Article, §18-207(b)(1), Annotated Code of Maryland, including negative results;
A qualitative or quantitative HIV viral load test result, including undetectable;
A CD4+ cell test result, count, or percentage in an individual not known to be HIV negative; or
An HIV genotype sequence test result.
Confidentiality.
A report required under this chapter is:
Confidential;
Not open to public inspection;
Not a medical record under Health-General Article, Title 4, Subtitle 3, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
Subject to the confidentiality requirements of Health-General Article, Title 4, Subtitle 1, Annotated Code of Maryland.
The reports and any proceedings, records, or files relating to the reports required under this chapter are not discoverable and are not admissible in evidence in any civil action.

Section C(1) of this regulation does not apply to a disclosure by the Secretary to another governmental agency performing its lawful duties pursuant to State or federal law where the Secretary determines that the agency to whom the information is disclosed will maintain the confidentiality of the disclosure.
Applicability.
The provisions of this regulation do not apply to the following:
Tar heaters;
Coke ovens;
Operations subject to the provisions of COMAR 26.11.08, 26.11.09, 26.11.11, 26.11.13, and 26.11.19.02G;
Installations or sources that are subject to COMAR 26.11.19, beginning with Regulation .03; and
Installations subject to an emissions limitation which specifies the lowest achievable emission rate for VOC under COMAR 26.11.17.03B(2).

The requirements of §B of this regulation apply to any person that discharges or causes to be discharged VOC with a vapor pressure greater than 0.002 pound per square inch (0.013 kilonewton/square meter) absolute.
Control of VOC from Installations.
The following requirements apply in Baltimore City and Anne Arundel, Baltimore, Carroll, Harford, Howard, Montgomery, and Prince George's counties:

Installations Constructed Before May 12, 1972. A person may not cause or permit the discharge of VOC from any installation constructed before May 12, 1972 in excess of 200 pounds (90.7 kilograms) per day unless the discharge is reduced by 85 percent or more overall.

Installations Constructed On or After May 12, 1972. Except as provided in §E of this regulation, a person may not cause or permit the discharge of VOC from any installation constructed on or after May 12, 1972, in excess of 20 pounds (9.07 kilograms) per day unless the discharge is reduced by 85 percent or more overall.
The following requirements apply in Calvert, Cecil, Charles, and Frederick counties:

Installations Constructed Before November 15, 1992. A person may not cause or permit the discharge of VOCs from any installation constructed before November 15, 1992 in excess of 200 pounds (90.7 kilograms) per day unless the discharge is reduced by 85 percent or more overall.

A person who owns or operates an existing installation that is not in compliance with §B(2)(a) of this regulation shall submit a plan for compliance for approval by the Department. The plan for compliance shall be submitted not later than April 15, 1993 and include an expeditious schedule of compliance so that compliance is achieved not later than May 15, 1995.

Installations Constructed On or After November 15, 1992. Except as provided in §E of this regulation, a person may not cause or permit the discharge of VOC from any installation constructed on or after November 15, 1992 in excess of 20 pounds (9.07 kilograms) per day unless the discharge is reduced by 85 percent or more overall.
Compliance with §B(1) and (2) of this regulation may be achieved by the following:
Use of an air pollution control system that is demonstrated to achieve 85 percent overall control, using approved test methods as referenced in COMAR 26.11.19;
Use of waterborne materials that have a total VOC content of less than 25 percent by volume as applied; or
Other operating or production limitations or process modifications that maintain emissions below the applicable limits established in §B(1) and (2) of this regulation.

VOC-Water Separators. A person may not cause or permit the discharge of VOC from single or multiple compartment VOC-water separators that receive effluent water containing 200 gallons (757 liters) of VOC or more per day with a true vapor pressure of 1.5 psi (10 kilonewtons/square meter) or greater unless one or more of the following vapor control devices are properly installed and operated:

A floating roof resting on the surface of the liquid equipped with a closure seal, or seals, to close the space between the roof edge and tank wall. All tank gauging and sampling devices shall be gas tight except when in use.

A vapor recovery system capable of collecting the VOC emitted from the tank and disposing of the VOC so as to prevent discharge to the atmosphere. All tank gauging and sampling devices shall be gas tight except when in use.
Other equally effective devices as approved by the Department.
VOC Disposal.
Applicability and Exemptions.
This section applies to any person that disposes of or treats waste containing VOC in the outside atmosphere in a manner that may cause evaporation of greater than 20 pounds per day.
This section does not apply to:
The operation of a municipal waste water treatment plant; and
The disposal of waste in a landfill in accordance with the requirements established by the Waste Management Administration.
General Requirements. A person may not treat or dispose of waste containing VOC in a manner that results in evaporation of greater than 20 pounds per day VOC to the atmosphere.
Exceptions.

A person who proposes to construct an installation that is not subject to NSR Source requirements in COMAR 26.11.17 and who is unable to comply with the requirements of §§B(1)(b) or B(2)(c) of this regulation, may request an exception from the Department.
A person may request an exception under this section by submitting an application to the Department in writing. A completed application for an exception shall include the following:
A detailed description of the proposed installation and any associated air pollution control devices;
A description of the intended use of the installation, its maximum rated capacity, and the capacity for which a permit is requested, if different from the maximum rated capacity;
A description of VOC emission points and an estimate of the VOC emissions from each emission point, along with calculations and supporting data;

A description of the air pollution control methods or equipment that may be available to achieve compliance with §§B(1)(b) or B(2)(c) of this regulation, the cost of the equipment or control methods, and information showing why the control methods are not reasonable for this installation;
A description of the operating procedures, production rate controls, or other methods that shall be used to meet an alternative VOC emission standard if an exception is granted; and
Other information that is requested by the Department and that relates to the Department's determination to grant or deny an exception to the requirements of §§B(1)(b) or B(2)(c) of this regulation.
The Department may grant an exception to §§B(1)(b) or B(2)(c) of this regulation if it determines that:
Control methods, if any, necessary to meet the requirements of §§B(1)(b) or B(2)(c) are not reasonable for the installation;

The applicant has the ability to operate and maintain the equipment and has the production controls necessary to meet the alternative VOC emission standard established by the Department instead of the requirements of §§B(1)(b) or B(2)(c); and
Emissions from the installation will not interfere with reasonable further progress if the exception is granted.
Procedures to be Used to Process an Application for an Exception and to Establish an Alternative Emission Standard.
The Department will acknowledge receipt of an application for an exception within 10 working days after receiving the application.
Not later than 90 days after receiving a completed application for an exception, the Department will complete its review and either:
Deny the request for an exception because it does not meet the requirements of §E(3) of this regulation; or
Make a determination to proceed with the exception and establish an alternative VOC emission standard in accordance with the procedures in §E(4)(c)—(h) of this regulation.

A determination to proceed with the exception will be proposed as a departmental order and permit to construct setting forth the alternative VOC emission standard, including any conditions determined by the Department to be necessary.

An applicant who is aggrieved by the Department's determination may request a contested case hearing before the Department. Any hearing under this paragraph will be held in accordance with the State Administrative Procedure Act.

If the applicant does not request reconsideration of the Department's determination to proceed with an exception, the Department will schedule a public hearing on the exception. The applicant shall publish a prominent advertisement in at least one newspaper of general circulation in the area concerned notifying the public of the hearing. The notice shall be made at the applicant's expense and in a form stipulated by the Department.
The notice shall be placed not later than 10 days after the Department makes its determination to proceed with the exception and at least 30 days before the scheduled hearing.
The hearing required by §E(4)(e) of this regulation satisfies the requirement for an opportunity for public comment or hearing on the permit to construct application under COMAR 26.11.02.09—.14.

If no information is presented at the hearing that would affect the issuance of the exception, and if the Department has no other basis for denying the exception, the Order and the permit will be issued not later than 10 days after the conclusion of the hearing.
The Department shall submit an exception to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency for approval as a revision to the State Implementation Plan.
